text: The surgeon demonstrates the inframesocolic approach, showing the splenic flexure and left mesocolon. Dissection begins with the opening of the left mesocolon near the inferior mesenteric vein, identifying and preserving the Toldt's fascia. The left mesocolon is elevated during dissection, facilitating mobilization of the colonic splenic flexure to prevent anastomotic tension during planned sigmoid or rectal resection.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the transverse mesocolon, leaving the left kidney and Gerota's fascia posteriorly. The dissection reaches the left paracolic gutter, where the pancreatic body is identified. The transverse colon is sectioned using a coagulating hook above the pancreatic body, allowing access to the lesser sac. The dissection proceeds along the pancreatic body, reaching and completely freeing the pancreatic tail.

The spleen is visible as the surgeon continues dissection towards the left paracolic gutter, freeing the left mesocolon and the left extremity of the transverse mesocolon. The flexure mobilization proceeds with the lateral opening of the left paracolic gutter, facilitated by the pneumoperitoneum reaching the lateral abdominal wall. Transparent parietocolic fibers are sectioned, revealing the spleen. The gastrocolic ligament is sectioned using a coagulating hook, exposing the second layer, which is also sectioned. The splenic flexure is mobilized, displaying the gyrotous fascia, pancreatic body and tail, spleen, and stomach. The colon is moved downward, and a final view through the mesocolic window revisits the organs.

The surgeon employs a coagulating hook to mobilize the gastrocolic ligament near the transverse colon, sequentially cutting through four distinct tissue layers. This dissection provides access to the lesser sac. The left paracolic gutter is sectioned, connecting to the previous dissection site, and Girota's fascia becomes visible.

The surgeon sections the attachments to the transverse mesocolon, reaching the beginning of the left mesocolon to be freed. The dissection continues medially, incising the paracolic gutter and freeing the colon while keeping the parietal fatty tissue laterally. The mobilization proceeds downward, re-exposing Girota's fascia and the left mesocolon. The left mesocolon, Girota's fascia, and the parietal wall are clearly identified. The left mesocolon is freed from its lateral attachments, exposing the pancreatic body. The mobilization of the left paracolic gutter continues, with careful dissection close to the left colon to avoid opening the parietal peritoneum. An incorrect dissection plane is shown with the opening of the parietal peritoneal sheet. The splenic flexure appears mobilized, allowing the entire left colon to be moved downward. Girota's fascia is prominently visible. If needed, the transverse mesocolon can be further freed from the pancreatic body for additional length distally.
The gastric wall, lesser sac, and pancreatic body are clearly displayed. The colon is visibly moved downward. The surgical approach transitions to a lateral to medial technique.

The surgeon notes a high splenic flexure and begins the procedure by incising the left paracolic gutter with a coagulating hook. The left mesocolon is displayed and freed from the parietal wall, revealing Girota's fascia. Incision of the tissue just above Girota's fascia exposes the spleen, and the fatty tissue is freed from the parietal-peritoneal sheath.

The surgeon mobilizes the gastrocolic ligament, staying close to the colon, and sections multiple layers to open the lesser sac, revealing the posterior wall of the stomach. The gastrocolic ligament is further freed, and attachments to the pancreatic body are sectioned. The view shows the mobilized splenic flexure, the downloaded colon, Girota's fascia, the pancreatic body, and the posterior wall of the stomach. The transverse colon appears well freed, and the splenic flexure is repositioned to its original region for demonstration.
